description: Recording revenue for billable hours worked on a client project during the month.
explanation: For service firms, revenue is recognized as the work is performed. If 20 hours were worked at $150/hr, $3,000 in revenue is recognized, regardless of when the client pays the invoice.

title: Prepaid Corporate Gym Membership
description: Paying for a 1-year fitness membership for employees as part of a wellness program.
explanation: Since the benefit is for the whole year, the payment is an asset. Each month, $100 will be moved to 'Employee Benefits Expense'.

title: Step Acquisition (Equity to Control)
description: Moving an investment from 'Associate' (20%) to 'Subsidiary' (60%) after buying more shares.
explanation: Under IFRS 3, when you gain control, you re-measure your previous stake at fair value and combine it with the new purchase to record the total investment in the subsidiary.

title: Unrestricted Cash Donation
description: Recording a cash contribution received for general operating purposes (without donor restrictions).
explanation: This is the most common entry for NGOs. The funds are available for any use the board deems necessary.

title: Donation Restricted for Specific Project
description: Recording cash received that is legally restricted by the donor for a specific program or purpose.
explanation: Under FASB/IFRS, you must track restricted revenue separately to ensure the money is only spent on the donor's specified project.

title: Unconditional Promise to Give (Pledge)
description: Recording a written promise from a donor to contribute cash in the future.
explanation: Pledges are recognized as revenue the moment the promise is made, provided it is unconditional. They are usually restricted by time until the cash is collected.

title: Release of Net Assets from Restriction
description: Moving funds from restricted to unrestricted once the project conditions have been satisfied.
explanation: This 'reclassification' entry is unique to NGOs. It shows that the organization has fulfilled the donor's wishes and can now 'use' the revenue on the income statement.

title: In-Kind Donation of Goods (Gifts-in-Kind)
description: Recording non-cash donations such as food, clothing, or equipment.
explanation: Goods should be recorded at their Fair Market Value (FMV). This reflects the true scale of the NGO's resources even if no cash was exchanged.

title: Contributed Specialized Services
description: Recording professional services (e.g., Legal or IT) provided for free by a professional.
explanation: You only record services if they create/enhance a non-financial asset or require specialized skills that the NGO would otherwise have to pay for.

title: Conditional Grant (Refundable Advance)
description: Recording a grant that has a 'barrier' or condition that must be met before revenue can be recognized.
explanation: If a grant says 'You only keep this if you vaccinate 1,000 children,' the money is a liability (Refundable Advance) until the work is done.

title: Allocating Salaries to Program Services
description: Dividing staff costs between direct mission work and administrative overhead.
explanation: Donors look at the 'Program Expense Ratio.' This entry ensures costs are accurately attributed to the mission vs. management.

title: Establishing a Permanent Endowment
description: Recording a gift where the principal must be kept intact forever.
explanation: The principal is restricted forever. Usually, the investment income generated by this cash is used for operations.

title: Allowance for Uncollectible Pledges
description: Estimating the portion of promised donations that will likely not be fulfilled.
explanation: Like a business's Bad Debt, this follows the conservatism principle to avoid overstating the value of future donations.

title: Fundraising Event Revenue & Expense
description: Recording gross ticket sales for a gala and the direct costs associated with the event.
explanation: NGOs must report the gross amount raised and the direct costs to donors (like the meal cost) to show the net efficiency of the fundraiser.

title: Board-Designated Reserve Fund
description: Internally restricting unrestricted cash for a future capital project.
explanation: Even if there is no donor restriction, the Board can 'designate' funds for a specific use (like a rainy-day fund). This is still technically 'Unrestricted' but labeled differently for clarity.

title: Agency Transaction (Pass-Through)
description: Recording funds received where the NGO acts as an agent/intermediary for another specified beneficiary.
explanation: If a donor says 'Give this to John Smith,' the NGO is just a middleman. No revenue is recognized; it is a liability until the cash is handed over.

title: Membership Dues (Exchange vs. Contribution)
description: Splitting membership fees between the value of services provided and the purely charitable portion.
explanation: If a member gets a $50 benefit (like a magazine or club access), only the amount above that fair value ($100) is considered a donation.

title: Unrelated Business Income Tax (UBIT)
description: Accruing tax for income generated from activities not substantially related to the NGO's exempt purpose.
explanation: If an NGO runs a profitable coffee shop or parking lot, that specific income is taxable at corporate rates to prevent unfair competition with for-profit businesses.

title: Endowment Investment Fair Value Adjustment
description: Adjusting the value of stocks/bonds held in an endowment fund at fiscal year-end.
explanation: Most endowment income is restricted until 'appropriated for spend' by the Board. This entry keeps the asset balance in line with the stock market.

title: Joint Costs: Program vs. Fundraising
description: Allocating costs for a direct mail campaign that includes both an educational message and a solicitation.
explanation: Under SOP 98-2, you can only allocate part of the cost to 'Program' if you meet specific criteria regarding Purpose, Audience, and Content.

title: Charitable Gift Annuity (Initial Recognition)
description: Recording a donor's gift where the NGO must pay the donor a fixed income for life.
explanation: The NGO receives $50k. However, the Present Value of what they must pay back to the donor over their lifetime is $30k. Only the remaining $20k is a donation today.

title: Capital Campaign (Building Fund)
description: Recording donations specifically restricted by the donor for the construction of a new facility.
explanation: Capital campaign funds are restricted until the building is built. Once construction costs are incurred, the net assets are 'released' to match the depreciation of the building.

title: Expiration of a Term Endowment
description: Releasing funds from restriction because a specific timeframe (e.g., 20 years) has passed.
explanation: Unlike permanent endowments, term endowments allow the principal to become unrestricted after a certain date or event.

title: Donated Use of Facilities (Free Rent)
description: Recording the fair market value of office space provided to the NGO for free by a landlord.
explanation: If a landlord gives you free space, you must record the expense and the revenue at Fair Market Value. This shows donors the 'true cost' of running your programs.

title: Charity Care (NGO Healthcare)
description: Recording medical services provided to a patient who has no ability to pay.
explanation: Charity care is not 'Bad Debt.' It is an intentional decision to provide service for free. It is recorded as a direct reduction of Gross Revenue.

title: Tuition Discount (Scholarship/Fellowship)
description: Recording a scholarship granted to a student in an NGO-run school or university.
explanation: Scholarships are not expenses; they are 'allowances' that reduce the gross tuition revenue to the net amount the school actually expects to collect.

title: Grant Sub-award to Local Partner
description: Recording the transfer of grant funds to a smaller NGO (sub-recipient) to execute a project.
explanation: International NGOs often 'pass through' money to local organizations. The lead NGO remains responsible for the audit trail of these funds.

title: Purchase of Museum Collection Item
description: Recording the acquisition of a piece of art or a historical artifact (if the NGO capitalizes its collection).
explanation: Many museums choose NOT to capitalize collections. If they do, collection items are recorded as assets but are generally NOT depreciated because they have an indefinite life.

title: Foreign Field Office Remittance (INGOs)
description: Sending funds from the home office to a foreign field office in a different currency.
explanation: The money is still an asset of the NGO, just in a different location. Exchange rate gains/losses will be recorded when the field office reports its monthly spending.

title: Inter-fund Transfer (General to Scholarship)
description: Internally moving cash between two different funds within the same legal entity.
explanation: Inter-fund transfers do not change the total Net Assets of the NGO; they just move money between internal 'buckets' for tracking purposes.

title: Museum Deaccessioning (Sale of Art)
description: Recording the sale of a collection item where proceeds are legally restricted for new acquisitions.
explanation: In museum accounting (AAM standards), money from selling art cannot be used for operations. It is recorded as restricted revenue and can only be used to buy other items for the collection.

title: Donor-Advised Fund (DAF) Contribution
description: Recording a contribution where the donor retains 'advisory privileges' over distributions.
explanation: Legally, the NGO owns the money, but for accounting purposes, it is tracked in a separate 'bucket' where the donor suggests the grants. It is usually treated as 'unrestricted' but 'designated' for that donor's advice.

title: Donated Life Insurance Policy
description: Recording the change in ownership of a life insurance policy given to the NGO.
explanation: If a donor gives the NGO their policy, the NGO records the 'Cash Surrender Value' (the amount the insurance company would pay if canceled today) as an asset.

title: Government Cost-Reimbursement Billing
description: Recognizing revenue after spending money on a government-funded project that pays on a reimbursement basis.
explanation: Many government grants are not paid upfront. You spend your own cash first (Expense), then this entry recognizes the revenue and the receivable from the government for that exact amount.

title: Beneficial Interest in Perpetual Trust
description: Recording the fair value of an interest in a trust held by a third party (like a bank).
explanation: The NGO doesn't have the cash; a bank does. But because the NGO is the sole beneficiary of the interest forever, it must record the fair value of the trust's assets on its own balance sheet.

title: Volunteer Stipend (Non-Employee Allowance)
description: Recording a small daily allowance or travel stipend paid to a volunteer (not a salary).
explanation: Stipends are not wages. They are typically recorded as a program expense. In many jurisdictions, these are tax-exempt if they only cover the volunteer's out-of-pocket costs.

title: Release of Donor Restriction Matching Depreciation
description: Releasing a portion of a restricted capital gift to match the annual depreciation of the purchased asset.
explanation: If a donor gives $10k for a machine with a 10-year life, you release $1k each year as 'Unrestricted' to offset the $1k depreciation expense on the Statement of Activities.

title: Multi-Year Grant (Present Value Discount)
description: Recording a long-term pledge and adjusting for the time value of money (PV).
explanation: If a donor promises $10k/year for 3 years, the money arriving in Year 3 is worth less today. You must record the receivable net of the discount to follow GAAP/IFRS.

title: Guest Advance Deposit (Booking)
description: Recording a cash deposit received from a guest for a future stay.
explanation: Until the guest actually stays, the money is a liability. It is held in the 'Guest Ledger' (or City Ledger) until check-in.

title: Daily Night Audit: Room Revenue
description: The daily entry to recognize revenue from guests currently in-house.
explanation: Every night, the auditor 'posts' the room rates to the guest folios. This recognizes the revenue for that day and increases the amount the guests owe.

title: Daily Night Audit: Incidental Charges
description: Recording charges for laundry, phone, or movie rentals posted to guest rooms.
explanation: Incidentals are secondary revenue streams. They are posted to the room folio and collected at check-out.

title: Guest Check-out (Credit Card Payment)
description: Clearing the guest folio when they pay by card at the front desk.
explanation: Check-out moves the balance from the 'Guest Ledger' (people in the building) to 'Credit Cards Receivable' (money coming from the bank).

title: Transfer to City Ledger (Direct Billing)
description: Moving a guest balance to City Ledger when a corporate client is billed directly.
explanation: If a guest is staying on a company account, they don't pay at checkout. Their balance is transferred to the 'City Ledger' to be invoiced to the corporation later.

title: Guaranteed No-Show Revenue
description: Recognizing revenue for a pre-paid room when the guest fails to arrive.
explanation: This converts the guest's advance deposit into revenue because the cancellation policy allows the hotel to keep the funds.

title: Front Desk Cash Float Establishment
description: Setting up the initial cash drawer for the receptionists to make change.
explanation: Each 'cashier' has a fixed float. This is a permanent asset on the balance sheet unless the drawer size is increased or decreased.

title: Night Audit Cash Shortage
description: Adjusting for a discrepancy when the actual cash collected is less than the POS report.
explanation: Hospitality requires daily cash reconciliation. Small errors are expensed immediately to keep the books matching the physical cash.

title: Guest Currency Exchange Service
description: Recording a transaction where the hotel exchanges USD for local currency for a guest.
explanation: Many hotels provide currency exchange as a service, making a small margin on the exchange rate.

title: Early Departure Penalty Fee
description: Charging a guest for leaving their stay earlier than the booked dates.
explanation: If a guest leaves early, the contract often allows the hotel to charge for one extra night as a penalty.

title: OTA Merchant Sale (Expedia Model)
description: Recording a booking where the guest pays the OTA, and the OTA pays the hotel net of commission.
explanation: Never record only the $80 as revenue. To track performance (RevPAR), you must record the full $100 and treat the $20 as a distribution expense.

title: OTA Agency Commission Accrual (Booking.com)
description: Accruing the commission expense for guests who stayed during the month but haven't been invoiced by the OTA yet.
explanation: In the Agency model, guests pay the hotel directly. The hotel must estimate the commission owed to Booking.com at month-end to match the revenue.

title: GDS Transaction Fee Recording
description: Recording the per-reservation fee charged by systems like Sabre, Amadeus, or Travelport.
explanation: GDS fees are 'transactional' costs of sales. They are recorded as operating expenses under the Rooms department.

title: Wholesale / Tour Operator Billing
description: Recording room revenue for a tour operator at a pre-negotiated 'Net' rate.
explanation: Wholesale rates are usually 'Net' of commission already. Unlike OTAs, these are often recorded at the contract rate because the hotel never sees the 'retail' price the guest paid the operator.

title: Credit Card Merchant Fee (Direct Deduction)
description: Recording the bank's commission on guest credit card payments.
explanation: When the bank settles credit card batches, they take a percentage (e.g., 2.5%). This is an administrative operating expense.

title: Travel Agent Commission Payout (Direct)
description: Paying a local travel agency for a group booking they brought to the hotel.
explanation: This clears the liability created when the group checked in and the commission was originally recognized as an expense.

title: OTA Accelerator / Marketing Fee
description: Recording the extra commission paid to an OTA to boost the hotel's search ranking.
explanation: Extra commissions for ranking boosts are often categorized under Marketing rather than Departmental Commission to keep cost-of-sales analysis clean.

title: Guest 'Walk' Expense (Overbooking)
description: Recording the cost of sending a guest to another hotel due to an overbooking error.
explanation: If the hotel is full, it must pay for the guest's room at a competitor's hotel. This is an operational loss in the Rooms department.

title: OTA Commission Reversal (No-Show)
description: Reversing an accrued commission because an OTA guest did not show up and was not charged.
explanation: If you don't charge the guest, the OTA doesn't get a commission. You must reverse your month-end accrual to keep expenses accurate.

title: Channel Manager Software Subscription
description: Monthly fee for the software that syncs rates across Expedia, Booking.com, and the Website.
explanation: This is a fixed operating cost. Under USALI, software specific to a department is charged to that department's 'Other Expenses' line.

title: Restaurant Sale (Charge to Room)
description: Recording a guest meal that is posted to their room folio rather than paid in cash.
explanation: This moves the transaction from the Restaurant POS to the Front Office system. The service charge is a liability because it must be distributed to staff.

title: Distribution of Staff Service Charge
description: Recording the payout of collected service charges to employees via payroll.
explanation: In many regions, the 10% service charge is mandatory and belongs to the staff. It is not an expense for the hotel; it is a pass-through liability.

title: Internal Transfer: Staff Meals
description: Moving the cost of food from the F&B department to the HR/General Admin department for employee dining.
explanation: To keep the 'Cost of Goods Sold' (COGS) accurate for the restaurant, the cost of feeding employees is removed from F&B and charged to the departments that the employees work in.

title: Daily Mini-Bar Revenue Posting
description: Recording the consumption of snacks and drinks from the guest room mini-bar.
explanation: Mini-bar revenue is usually tracked as a sub-category of F&B. It is posted manually by housekeeping or via automated sensors.

title: F&B Wastage & Spoilage Recording
description: Adjusting inventory for food that was prepared for a buffet but not consumed and cannot be reused.
explanation: Tracking wastage separately from COGS allows the Chef to monitor kitchen efficiency and over-production.

title: Complimentary Breakfast (Room Package)
description: Internal accounting to credit the F&B department for 'free' breakfasts included in a room rate.
explanation: If a room is $100 including breakfast, the auditor must 're-route' the value of the breakfast ($15) to F&B so that the restaurant gets credit for the sale.

title: Purchase of Beverage Inventory (Alcohol)
description: Recording the bulk purchase of wines and spirits for the hotel bar.
explanation: Liquor is a high-value asset. It stays on the balance sheet until it is issued to the bar, at which point it becomes a Cost of Sale.

title: Crockery & Glassware Breakage
description: Expensing broken plates, glasses, and cutlery from the restaurant floor.
explanation: Crockery is often handled as an operating supply. Regular inventory counts of 'par stock' determine the monthly breakage expense.

title: Banquet Deposit Recognition (Event Day)
description: Converting a wedding or conference deposit into revenue once the event occurs.
explanation: Banquet deposits are held as liabilities for months. On the day of the event, the revenue is finally earned.

title: Kitchen Fuel (LPG/Gas) Accrual
description: Recording the estimated cost of gas used in the kitchen before the utility bill arrives.
explanation: Under USALI, energy costs directly attributable to a department (like gas for a kitchen) should be tracked separately if possible.

title: Meeting Room Rental Revenue
description: Recording the pure rental fee for a conference hall or ballroom.
explanation: Unlike F&B, room rental has almost zero cost of goods sold. It is classified under 'Other Operated Departments' or a sub-category of Events in USALI.

title: AV Equipment Hire (Outsourced)
description: Recording the revenue and cost when the hotel hires AV equipment from a 3rd party for a guest.
explanation: The hotel acts as a principal here, marking up the service. Gross revenue and gross cost must be shown to track the margin on third-party services.

title: Event Cancellation Fee
description: Recognizing revenue when a client cancels a wedding or event and forfeits their deposit.
explanation: Since the event won't happen, the liability is 'cleared' to revenue. This is usually classified as 'Other Income' because no actual service was provided.

title: Group Attrition Fees (Room Block)
description: Charging a corporate group for failing to fill the minimum number of rooms promised in their contract.
explanation: Attrition is a penalty for under-performance. It compensates the hotel for 'displacement' (rooms that could have been sold to others).

title: Vendor Commission (Kickback Income)
description: Recording a commission received from a florist or photographer for recommending them to a wedding couple.
explanation: This is common in the events industry. The hotel earns a 'referral fee' which is recorded as non-operating miscellaneous income.

title: Delegate Day Rate (DDR) Splitting
description: Splitting a single package price (e.g., $75 per person) between room rental and food.
explanation: To keep departmental performance (GOP) accurate, a 'bundled' package must be manually split based on the internal value of the components.

title: Transfer to Master Account (Group Billing)
description: Moving individual guest room charges to a single 'Master Folio' to be paid by the corporate sponsor.
explanation: In a group booking, guests don't pay individually. The night auditor 'slabs' all individual charges into one City Ledger account for bulk billing.

title: Exhibition Booth Space Rental
description: Recording revenue from vendors who rent small areas in the lobby or foyer for displays.
explanation: This is separate from meeting room hire and covers 'non-traditional' spaces used for commercial display.

title: Internal Departmental Meeting (Cross-Charge)
description: Charging the Marketing department for holding their annual strategy meeting in a hotel conference room.
explanation: While this nets to zero for the whole hotel, it ensures that the Events department gets 'credit' for the space used and Marketing is held accountable for their budget.

title: Banquet Casual Labor Accrual
description: Recording the cost of temp workers hired specifically for a one-night gala event.
explanation: Banquets often use agency staff. This cost should be tracked specifically under the Events department rather than General Payroll to monitor the 'Cost of Labor' for events.

title: Base Management Fee Accrual
description: Recording the monthly fee paid to the hotel management company (usually % of Total Revenue).
explanation: Management companies typically charge 2-4% of Total Revenue as a base fee to run the hotel on behalf of the owner.

title: Incentive Management Fee
description: Recording the performance-based fee paid to management based on GOP targets.
explanation: This is a 'Success Fee.' It is only recorded if the hotel achieves a certain level of Gross Operating Profit (GOP), rewarding the manager for efficiency.

title: Franchise Royalty Fee Payment
description: Recording the monthly royalty fee paid to the brand for using their name and systems.
explanation: Franchise fees are usually a percentage of Room Revenue only. This is separate from management fees.

title: Guest Loyalty Program Assessment
description: Recording the cost the hotel pays to the brand when a guest earns points during their stay.
explanation: When a guest earns 'Points,' the individual hotel must pay a fee to the global brand's loyalty fund. This is a cost of guest acquisition.

title: Loyalty Point Redemption (Reimbursement)
description: Recording revenue when a guest stays for 'Free' using points and the brand reimburses the hotel.
explanation: The hotel doesn't get the full room rate; the brand pays a lower 'reimbursement rate' (often based on occupancy levels) to cover the cost of the free stay.

title: Linen and Terry Amortization
description: Monthly expense for the gradual wear and tear of bedsheets and towels.
explanation: In hotels, linen isn't 'inventory' that is sold; it is an operating asset. It is capitalized and then amortized (expensed) over its useful life (usually 12-24 months).

title: FF&E Reserve Funding (Capex)
description: Setting aside cash for future replacement of Furniture, Fixtures & Equipment.
explanation: Banks and Management Agreements usually require the owner to set aside 3-5% of Total Revenue into a separate account to pay for future renovations.

title: Complimentary Room for Influencer/PR
description: Internal tracking of the cost of providing a free room for marketing purposes.
explanation: You don't record revenue for a comp room. Instead, you move the 'cost' (cleaning, laundry, utilities) out of the Rooms department and into Marketing.

title: Outsourced Laundry Service Bill
description: Recording the monthly cost of an external company washing the hotel's linen.
explanation: If the hotel doesn't have an internal 'On-Premise Laundry' (OPL), this is a significant recurring operating cost for the Rooms department.

title: Global Marketing Fund Assessment
description: Recording the fee paid to the brand to fund global TV ads and the central website.
explanation: Most brands charge a fixed % of room revenue to pay for the 'Big Picture' marketing that individual hotels cannot afford on their own.

title: Initial Purchase of Livestock
description: Recording the purchase of young animals for breeding or eventual sale.
explanation: Livestock is recorded as a biological asset. Under IAS 41, it should ideally be recorded at fair value minus costs to sell.

title: Recognition of New Births (Progeny)
description: Recording the birth of calves, lambs, or chicks which increases the herd size.
explanation: When an animal is born, you gain an asset for 'free.' You record the fair value of the newborn immediately as income.

title: Biological Growth (Physical Change)
description: Adjusting the value of animals as they get older, heavier, and more valuable.
explanation: IAS 41 requires you to separate growth into 'Price Change' and 'Physical Change.' This entry records the value added because the animals grew larger.

title: Livestock Maintenance (Feed & Vet)
description: Recording the day-to-day costs of keeping the animals healthy.
explanation: Unlike manufacturing, you generally do NOT capitalize feed and vet costs into the asset value. They are expensed as incurred because growth is captured via the Fair Value adjustment.

title: Death of Livestock (Loss)
description: Removing an animal from the books due to disease or accident.
explanation: When an animal dies, the entire fair value of that asset is written off as an immediate loss.

title: Sale of Live Animals
description: Recording the revenue from selling a live animal to another farmer or market.
explanation: The biological asset is removed at its current book (fair) value. Any difference between the sale price and book value is a gain or loss.

title: Government Grant (Unconditional)
description: Recording a subsidy received from the government related to biological assets.
explanation: Under IAS 41, an unconditional grant related to biological assets measured at fair value is recognized as income the moment it becomes receivable.

title: Planting Bearer Plants (Orchard/Vineyard)
description: Recording the cost of planting fruit trees or vines that will produce for many years.
explanation: Under IAS 16, bearer plants are treated like Fixed Assets (Machinery). You capitalize the cost of planting and tending to them until they reach maturity.

title: Depreciation of Bearer Plants
description: Annual depreciation of fruit trees over their productive life.
explanation: Because bearer plants are PPE, they must be depreciated over their useful life (e.g., 20 years for an orange grove).

title: Unharvested Crops (Standing Crops)
description: Recognizing the value of crops still in the field at year-end.
explanation: Crops in the field are biological assets. Even if not harvested, if they have grown, their increased value must be recognized as profit.

title: Harvesting Crops (Transformation)
description: The moment a crop is picked, it stops being a 'Biological Asset' and becomes 'Inventory'.
explanation: At the point of harvest, the produce is measured at Fair Value. This value becomes the 'Cost' for the inventory account going forward.

title: Land Preparation Costs
description: Costs for tilling, plowing, and preparing soil for a new season.
explanation: General soil preparation for annual crops is usually expensed in the period it occurs.

title: Purchase of Fertilizer/Seeds
description: Recording the bulk purchase of farming inputs.
explanation: These are not expenses until they are actually spread on the field or planted in the ground.

title: Post-Harvest Spoilage (Inventory)
description: Recording the loss of picked produce that rotted in storage.
explanation: Once harvested, the produce follows IAS 2 (Inventory) rules. Any spoilage is an immediate expense.

title: Shearing Sheep (Agricultural Produce)
description: Recording the harvest of wool from live sheep.
explanation: Wool is 'Agricultural Produce.' The gain is recognized at the moment of shearing at its fair value.

title: Livestock Slaughter (Biological to Inventory)
description: Moving an animal from the biological asset account to the meat inventory account.
explanation: The animal ceases to be a biological asset the moment it is slaughtered. It enters inventory at its fair value at the point of slaughter.

title: Daily Milk Production
description: Recording the fair value of milk collected from dairy cows.
explanation: Milk is produce. It is recorded as inventory at fair value minus costs to sell at the moment of milking.

title: Sale of Harvested Produce
description: Recording the final sale of stored grain, wool, or milk.
explanation: Once harvested, the sale follows standard retail/wholesale accounting rules.

title: Breeding & Artificial Insemination Fees
description: Paying for specialized breeding services to improve herd quality.
explanation: These are considered operating expenses unless they relate to a specific project to create a new type of biological asset.

title: Irrigation & Water Costs
description: Recording the heavy utility costs for crop watering.
explanation: A major operating cost in farming, tracked separately to analyze crop yield vs. water cost.

title: Initial Patient Billing (Gross Charges)
description: Recording the full 'chargemaster' price for medical services before insurance discounts.
explanation: Hospitals always bill the full list price first. This is called 'Gross Revenue' and is used to track the total volume of work performed.

title: Contractual Adjustment (Insurance Discount)
description: Adjusting the receivable to reflect the lower amount the insurance company is legally required to pay.
explanation: This is the most critical healthcare entry. It reduces revenue from 'Gross' to 'Net Realizable Value.' It represents the difference between the hospital's price and the insurance company's allowed rate.

title: Capitation Revenue (Fixed Fee per Member)
description: Recording a monthly fixed payment from an insurance company to cover all services for a group of members.
explanation: In managed care, the clinic gets paid a flat fee every month regardless of whether the patients show up or not. This shifts the financial risk from the insurer to the provider.

title: Third-Party Payer Settlement (True-up)
description: Adjusting the books at year-end when final insurance/government reimbursements are confirmed.
explanation: Insurance payments are often 'estimated' until a final audit is done by the payer. This entry 'trues-up' those estimates.

title: Provision for Uncollectible Patient Accounts
description: Estimating the amount of 'Self-Pay' balances that will likely never be paid.
explanation: Unlike 'Charity Care' (which you already have), Bad Debt is for patients who are *expected* to pay but don't. It is an operating expense.

title: Concierge Medicine Membership Fees
description: Recording an annual fee paid by a patient for priority access to a doctor.
explanation: Common in modern clinics. Since the service is provided over a year, the fee is a liability (unearned) and recognized at $100 per month.

title: Collection of Patient Co-payment
description: Recording the cash paid by the patient at the time of service (the 'out-of-pocket' portion).
explanation: The co-pay reduces the total receivable. The remaining balance will be billed to the insurance provider.

title: Medical Malpractice Insurance Premium
description: Accruing the monthly cost of professional liability insurance for doctors.
explanation: Malpractice insurance is one of the highest operating costs for clinics. It is usually paid annually but must be expensed monthly.

title: Physician Productivity Bonus (RVU Based)
description: Recording a bonus for a doctor based on their volume of 'Relative Value Units' (RVUs).
explanation: Most doctors aren't just on a flat salary; they get paid based on how many procedures they perform. This must be calculated and accrued at month-end.

title: Medical Equipment Maintenance (Bio-Med)
description: Recording the cost of calibrating and repairing specialized medical machines (MRI, X-ray).
explanation: Bio-medical engineering costs are unique to healthcare and are tracked separately from general building maintenance.

title: Pharmaceutical Volume Rebate
description: Recording a credit earned from a drug manufacturer for purchasing large quantities of medicine.
explanation: Rebates from drug companies reduce the hospital's cost of supplies, effectively increasing their margin on pharmacy sales.

title: Blood Bank Inventory Usage
description: Recording the cost of blood units used during a surgery.
explanation: Blood is a highly perishable and specialized inventory item. Its usage is tracked patient-by-patient.

title: Provision for Uninsured Malpractice Claims
description: Recording a liability for an 'Incurred But Not Reported' (IBNR) medical legal claim.
explanation: Hospitals must estimate and set aside funds for potential lawsuits that have occurred but haven't been filed yet (Self-insurance logic).

title: Usage of Consignment Implants (Joints/Stents)
description: Recording the purchase and immediate usage of a medical device owned by a vendor until the moment of surgery.
explanation: Hospitals often don't 'own' expensive implants (like artificial hips). They sit in the hospital, but the hospital only 'buys' them from the vendor the second they are put into a patient.

title: Patient Trust Fund Deposit (Nursing Home)
description: Recording cash held on behalf of a long-term care patient for their personal use.
explanation: The hospital acts as a bank for long-term patients. This is an agency transaction; the money does not belong to the hospital.

title: Medical Records Request Fee
description: Recording the administrative fee charged to lawyers or patients for copies of medical records.
explanation: This is incidental revenue generated by the Health Information Management (HIM) department.

title: Physician Recruitment Loan (Forgivable)
description: Recording an advance payment to a new doctor that is 'forgiven' if they stay for 3 years.
explanation: To attract doctors, hospitals give large cash sign-on bonuses. These are recorded as assets and then 'forgiven' (expensed) over several years of service.

title: Hospital Ancillary Revenue (Retail)
description: Recording daily sales from the hospital cafeteria or gift shop.
explanation: These are 'Other Operated Departments' that generate revenue from visitors and staff rather than patients.

title: Provision for Denied Claims
description: Recording a reserve for insurance claims that are expected to be rejected due to coding or eligibility errors.
explanation: Revenue Cycle Management (RCM) experts know that ~5-10% of claims get denied. This entry ensures you don't overstate your assets by accounting for those rejections before they happen.

title: Telehealth Platform Revenue (Net of Fee)
description: Recording revenue from a virtual visit where the platform provider takes a service fee.
explanation: Telehealth is often billed through third-party platforms. Like 'Talabat' for restaurants, you must record the Gross Revenue to track your clinical volume accurately.

title: Value-Based Care Quality Incentive
description: Recording an accrued bonus from an insurer for hitting patient health outcome targets.
explanation: Modern healthcare pays for 'Value' (quality) not just 'Volume.' If data shows you reduced patient readmissions, you earn a bonus that must be accrued.

title: Medicare Cost Report Settlement (Payable)
description: Recording a liability when a government audit determines the hospital was overpaid in the prior year.
explanation: Government payers (Medicare/Medicaid) pay 'interim' rates. At year-end, a 'Cost Report' is filed. If you were overpaid based on actual costs, you must record a liability to pay it back.

title: Graduate Medical Education (GME) Funding
description: Recording government subsidies received for training medical residents.
explanation: Teaching hospitals receive specific funding to offset the cost of salaries for doctors in training (Residents). This is a non-patient revenue stream.

title: Biohazardous Waste Disposal Expense
description: Recording the high cost of disposing of regulated medical waste (sharps, chemicals).
explanation: Medical waste cannot go in standard trash. The disposal is highly regulated and expensive, representing a significant operating cost for surgical centers.

title: EMR/EHR System Capitalization
description: Recording the multi-million dollar cost of implementing an Electronic Medical Record system (like Epic or Cerner).
explanation: EMR systems are the 'brain' of a hospital. Implementation costs (coding, licensing) are capitalized and amortized over 5-10 years.

title: Certificate of Need (CON) Intangible
description: Recording the legal and application costs to obtain a government permit to expand a hospital wing.
explanation: In many regions, you cannot simply open a hospital; you must prove the community 'needs' it. This permit (CON) is a valuable intangible asset.

title: 340B Drug Pricing Program Savings
description: Recording the purchase of outpatient drugs at a significant federal discount.
explanation: The 340B program allows safety-net hospitals to buy drugs at steep discounts. When these are sold at normal insurance rates, the hospital captures a high margin to fund charity care.

title: Home Healthcare Staff Travel Cost
description: Recording mileage and travel time for nurses providing care in patient homes.
explanation: For Home Health agencies, travel is a core operating cost. Unlike in-hospital care, 'windshield time' must be tracked and expensed to analyze visit profitability.

title: Clinical Trial Funding (Pharma Contract)
description: Recording an upfront payment from a pharmaceutical company to conduct a multi-year drug trial.
explanation: Teaching hospitals act as research centers. This money is a liability until the hospital hits specific 'milestones' (e.g., enrolling 100 patients), at which point it is recognized as revenue.

title: Elective Surgery Pre-payment
description: Recording a cash deposit for a non-emergency or cosmetic procedure before the surgery date.
explanation: For elective procedures, hospitals often require payment upfront. This is a liability until the surgery is performed and the clinical record is closed.

title: ACO Shared Savings Bonus Accrual
description: Accruing a bonus from a government/payer because the healthcare network reduced the total cost of care for a population.
explanation: Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) are rewarded for efficiency. If the hospital keeps patients healthy and out of the ER, the payer shares the 'savings' with the hospital.

title: Medical Office Building (MOB) Rental
description: Recording rent received from independent doctors who lease space in a hospital-owned building.
explanation: Large hospitals are also real estate companies. They lease 'MOB' space to private practices to ensure those doctors refer patients to the hospital's surgical center.

title: Funding a Self-Insurance Trust
description: Transferring cash into a restricted trust account to cover future employee health or malpractice claims.
explanation: Massive healthcare systems 'insure themselves.' They set up a legal trust to pay claims, which moves the cash from 'available' to 'restricted' status.

title: JV Equity Pickup (Imaging Center/Lab)
description: Recording the hospital's share of profit from a 50/50 joint venture partnership in a Diagnostic Imaging Center.
explanation: Hospitals often partner with private companies to run labs or MRI centers. Under the equity method, the hospital records its share of the venture's monthly profit.

title: Capital Lease of Robotic System (da Vinci)
description: Initial recognition of a surgical robot and the corresponding lease liability.
explanation: High-tech robots are often leased rather than bought. This IFRS 16/ASC 842 entry brings the $2M+ asset and debt onto the balance sheet.

title: Patient Overpayment Refund
description: Processing a cash refund to a patient when both the insurance and the patient overpaid the total bill.
explanation: If a patient's account has a credit balance (they paid $200 but insurance covered everything), the hospital is legally required to refund that cash.

title: IBNR (Incurred But Not Reported) Accrual
description: Estimating the cost of medical services that have been provided to employees but haven't been billed yet (Self-insured).
explanation: For hospitals that self-insure their staff, they must estimate 'hidden' claims that are currently in the mail or still being coded at the clinic.

title: Pharmacy Compounding (WIP to Finished)
description: Recording the internal labor and material costs to create a specialized chemotherapy IV bag.
explanation: In-patient pharmacies are like tiny factories. They combine raw drugs and labor to create a finished 'product' for a specific patient.

title: Freight Revenue Recognition (Bill of Lading)
description: Recording revenue for a shipment once the goods have been delivered or the contract terms are met.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, revenue is recognized when control of the service passes to the customer, usually upon confirmed delivery for short-haul or pro-rata for long-haul.

title: 3PL Monthly Storage Fees
description: Charging a client for warehouse space used during the month based on pallet count or square footage.
explanation: This is recurring service revenue for Third-Party Logistics (3PL) providers, typically billed at the end of every month.

title: Recording Actual Fuel Usage
description: Moving the cost of fuel from the prepaid fuel card to the operating expense account based on actual consumption.
explanation: Following your 'Fuel Card Replenishment' entry, this recognizes the actual expense when the truck is refilled.

title: Outsourced Freight (Sub-contractor)
description: Recording the cost of hiring a third-party driver or carrier to fulfill a customer order.
explanation: In 'Asset-Light' logistics, the company bills the client $2500 and pays a sub-contractor $1800, capturing a margin on the coordination.

title: Customs Duties (Agency Pass-through)
description: Recording the payment of government duties on behalf of a client.
explanation: Customs clearing agents do not record duties as an expense or revenue. It is a 'Balance Sheet only' transaction because they are just an intermediary for the client.

title: Goods-in-Transit (Marine) Insurance
description: Recording the insurance premium for a high-value international shipment.
explanation: This is a direct cost of the shipment (COGS) used to protect against cargo loss or damage during transit.

title: Terminal Handling Charges (THC)
description: Recording the fees paid to a port authority for loading/unloading containers.
explanation: These are mandatory costs at ports like Aqaba or Jebel Ali to move containers from the ship to the yard.

title: Fleet Tyre Replacement (Component Accounting)
description: Recording the bulk replacement of truck tyres which will last for more than one year.
explanation: Tyres are high-wear items. Large fleets treat them as inventory or a separate component of the truck asset and expense them based on mileage or time.

title: COD Collection (Last Mile Delivery)
description: Recording the cash collected from a customer at the door on behalf of an E-commerce vendor.
explanation: For companies like Aramex, the cash collected isn't theirs. It's a liability until they transfer it back to the merchant (less their delivery fee).

title: Truck Detention Fees (Customer Delay)
description: Charging a customer for keeping a driver waiting at the loading dock past the free-time limit.
explanation: Unlike Port Demurrage (which is an expense), this is revenue the logistics company earns when the *client* causes a delay.

title: Warehouse Shrinkage (3PL Responsibility)
description: Recording a loss when the warehouse loses or damages a client's inventory.
explanation: In 3PL contracts, the warehouse is liable for inventory accuracy. If items are missing, the warehouse must reimburse the client at cost.

title: Major Engine Overhaul (Capitalization)
description: Recording a significant engine rebuild that extends the truck's life by 5 years.
explanation: Routine oil changes are expensed. But a rebuild that restores the asset's utility is capitalized under IAS 16 and depreciated.

title: Highway Tolls & Weighbridge Fees
description: Recording the daily cash/tag costs for road usage and weight compliance.
explanation: These are immediate operating expenses necessary for every long-haul journey.

title: Fulfillment Labor (Picking & Packing)
description: Recording the direct labor cost associated with preparing 3PL orders for shipment.
explanation: For 3PL companies, warehouse labor is a 'Direct Cost' (COGS) rather than an administrative expense.

title: Shipping Line Container Deposit
description: Recording a refundable deposit paid to a shipping line to take a container out of the port.
explanation: This is an asset, not an expense. The money is returned once the empty container is returned to the shipping line's yard.

title: Deadhead / Empty Repositioning
description: Recording the fuel and labor cost of moving an empty truck to a new pickup location.
explanation: Deadheading (driving empty) is a major efficiency metric in logistics. These costs are expensed immediately but tracked for route optimization.

title: Cold Storage (Reefer) Electricity Accrual
description: Recording the estimated high-power utility cost for temperature-controlled warehousing.
explanation: Cold storage logistics have energy costs 10x higher than dry storage. This must be accrued to match the monthly storage revenue.

title: Fleet Accident Insurance Claim
description: Recording the expected reimbursement from insurance after a truck accident.
explanation: If a truck is damaged, you record a receivable for the amount the insurance company has agreed to pay to cover the repair or total loss.

title: Freight Forwarding Commission
description: Recording the fee earned for arranging transportation without touching the goods.
explanation: For agents who act as intermediaries, only the fee is revenue (Net Basis), not the total freight cost.

title: Telematics & GPS Subscription Fees
description: Monthly fee for fleet management and tracking software.
explanation: This is a fixed operating cost for modern fleets to ensure safety and route compliance.

title: Documentation & BL Fees
description: Recording revenue for preparing shipping documents and Bill of Ladings.
explanation: These small fees add up for freight forwarders and are recognized as soon as the documents are issued.

title: Annual Heavy Vehicle Road Tax
description: Paying the annual government registration fee for a commercial truck.
explanation: This is a prepaid asset that is amortized at $100 per month into the 'Taxes & Licenses' expense account.

title: Depreciation of Warehouse Racking
description: Annual depreciation of the specialized steel structures used for pallet storage.
explanation: Racking is a fixed asset with a typical life of 10-15 years. This ensures the cost of the warehouse setup is spread over its useful life.

title: Monthly Container Lease Payment
description: Recording the cost of renting shipping containers from a leasing company.
explanation: If the containers are not owned, the monthly lease is a direct operating expense of the shipping department.

title: Fuel Surcharge (Revenue)
description: Charging the client an additional variable fee to offset rising diesel/bunker prices.
explanation: Fuel surcharges are standard in logistics. They allow the carrier to protect margins without changing the base contract rate.

title: Freight Claim Subrogation
description: Recording the recovery of a damage claim from a sub-contractor's insurance company.
explanation: If your sub-contractor damages goods, you pay your client first (Expense) and then 'subrogate' (reclaim) the money from the sub-contractor's insurance.

title: LCL Consolidation (Margin Accounting)
description: Recording the revenue from 'Less than Container Load' clients versus the 'Full Container Load' cost paid to the ship.
explanation: This is the core profit model for Freight Forwarders: buying a whole container space and selling it in 'slices' to small shippers.

title: Bonded Warehouse (Deferred Tax Tracking)
description: Tracking the potential VAT/Duty liability for goods held in a bonded area (Memo Entry logic).
explanation: In a bonded warehouse, taxes aren't paid until goods leave. The 3PL doesn't own the goods, but they are legally responsible to the government for the tax if the goods go missing.

title: Intermodal Drayage (Short-haul)
description: Recording the cost of moving a container from a ship to a rail yard (Drayage).
explanation: Drayage is a specific niche of logistics focusing only on the short distances between ports, warehouses, and rail heads.

title: Bunker Adjustment Factor (BAF) Surcharge
description: Recording the oceanic fuel surcharge (Bunker) charged by the shipping line.
explanation: BAF is a fluctuating charge based on the cost of ship fuel (Bunker). It is almost always billed separately from the base ocean freight.

title: Reverse Logistics (Returns Handling)
description: Recording the service fee for processing a returned E-commerce item.
explanation: Handling returns is more labor-intensive than shipping. Shippers charge a 'Return Handling Fee' per item processed back into inventory.

title: Cargo Lien (Bad Debt Defense)
description: Reclassifying client inventory as a 'Lien' asset when the client fails to pay freight charges.
explanation: Under maritime and transport law, if a client doesn't pay, the logistics provider can legally hold (and eventually sell) the goods to recover the debt.

title: Demurrage Income (Company-Owned Containers)
description: Revenue earned by a container leasing or shipping line when a client keeps a container too long.
explanation: If you own the containers, the penalty paid by the client is your revenue (opposite of the Demurrage Expense entry).

title: Ship Chartering (Time Charter Payment)
description: Recording the massive pre-payment for 'renting' an entire ship for 30 days.
explanation: In high-level shipping, you don't book a container; you 'charter' the vessel. This is a prepaid expense that is amortized daily over the voyage.

title: Cross-Docking Service Revenue
description: Recording revenue for moving goods directly from an incoming truck to an outgoing truck without storage.
explanation: Cross-docking saves storage costs but requires high labor intensity. It is billed as a 'throughput' fee.

title: Reefer Monitoring (Cold Chain)
description: Charging for the specialized labor of checking temperatures on a cold-storage container every 4 hours.
explanation: In Pharma or Food logistics, 'Monitoring' is a premium service billed separately from the freight itself.

title: Empty Container Depot Storage
description: Charging a shipping line for storing their empty 'empties' in your yard.
explanation: Container yards make money not only on full goods but on the 'real estate' used by empty steel boxes waiting for their next voyage.

title: General Average Contribution (Maritime Law)
description: Recording a liability when a ship's captain sacrifices cargo or incurs extraordinary expense to save the vessel.
explanation: Under maritime law (York-Antwerp Rules), if a ship catches fire and some cargo is thrown overboard to save the rest, ALL cargo owners must split the cost. This is a rare but massive financial hit for shippers.

title: Canal Transit Tolls (Suez / Panama)
description: Accruing the massive fees required for a vessel to pass through a major international canal.
explanation: Canal tolls for large ships can cost hundreds of thousands of dollars. For shipping lines, these are specific 'Voyage Costs' that must be accrued the moment the ship enters the queue.

title: Fuel Price Hedge (Derivative Adjustment)
description: Adjusting the fair value of a fuel swap contract used to lock in diesel or bunker prices.
explanation: Logistics giants use derivatives to stay profitable if fuel prices spike. Under IFRS 9, these must be revalued monthly, usually through Other Comprehensive Income (OCI).

title: DDP Sale (Duty Paid Recognition)
description: Recording a sale under 'Delivered Duty Paid' where the seller is responsible for import taxes.
explanation: In DDP shipments, the logistics provider (or seller) absorbs the tax. Unlike a pass-through, the duty is a direct operating expense of the sale.

title: Stevedoring & Shore Handling Fees
description: Recording the cost of specialized dock labor used to load/unload a ship.
explanation: Stevedoring is a specialized labor category in port logistics. It is a variable cost that scales directly with tonnage or container count.

title: Maritime Salvage Award (Revenue)
description: Recording revenue earned for assisting a vessel in distress at sea.
explanation: Under international law, if your ship saves another ship, you are entitled to a 'Salvage Award' (a % of the value saved). This is recognized when the maritime court/arbitrator finalizes the award.

title: Carbon Emissions (ETS) Liability
description: Accruing the cost of carbon credits required for international shipping emissions (EU ETS logic).
explanation: Modern logistics (especially in Europe) must pay for their carbon footprint. This entry matches the cost of emissions to the month the voyage occurred.

title: Project Logistics: Mobilization Fee
description: Recording the cost of moving specialized heavy-lift cranes to a job site.
explanation: In 'Project Logistics' (moving windmills or factories), getting the equipment to the site (Mobilization) is a massive upfront cost that is capitalized as WIP and recognized as revenue over the project life.

title: Port Agency Disbursement (D/A)
description: Handling funds provided by a ship owner to an agent to pay for port expenses.
explanation: Port agents act as 'bankers' for ships. They receive a lump sum (Proforma D/A), pay the port, and return the balance. It is a pass-through on the balance sheet.

title: Reverse Factoring (Supplier Payment)
description: When a bank pays a logistics sub-contractor early on behalf of the main company.
explanation: In Supply Chain Finance (SCF), the logistics firm moves its debt from a 'Trade Payable' to a 'Bank Debt' because the bank paid the trucker immediately.

title: Issuing Raw Materials to Production
description: Moving the cost of materials from the warehouse to the assembly line (Work-in-Process).
explanation: This represents the moment raw steel or plastic is 'consumed' by a specific production job.

title: Usage of Indirect Materials (Factory Supplies)
description: Recording materials used in the factory that aren't part of the final product (e.g., lubricants, cleaning rags).
explanation: Indirect materials cannot be traced to a single unit, so they flow through the Overhead account instead of WIP.

title: Applying Manufacturing Overhead (Applied)
description: Allocating estimated indirect costs to products based on a predetermined rate (e.g., $5 per machine hour).
explanation: This is a core CMA concept. Since actual overhead isn't known until the bills arrive, we use an 'Applied' rate to cost products in real-time.

title: Transfer of WIP to Finished Goods (COGM)
description: Recording the completion of the manufacturing process.
explanation: This represents the 'Cost of Goods Manufactured'. The product is now ready for sale and sitting in the shipping warehouse.

title: Sale of Finished Goods (COGS Recognition)
description: Recording the cost of the product sold to a customer.
explanation: This follows the matching principle—expensing the production cost only when the revenue is earned.

title: Factory Utility Accrual (Electricity/Water)
description: Recording the actual cost of running the machines for the month.
explanation: Factory utilities are a component of actual overhead. They are compared against 'Applied Overhead' at month-end.

title: Factory Rent vs. Admin Office Rent
description: Splitting rent between production and administrative use.
explanation: Rent for the production floor is a product cost (Overhead), while rent for the sales office is a period cost (Expense).

title: Indirect Labor (Supervisor Salaries)
description: Recording wages for factory staff who don't touch the product directly.
explanation: Supervisor and maintenance salaries are 'productized' via overhead application, rather than being expensed directly like admin salaries.

title: Factory Equipment Depreciation
description: Recording the wear and tear of production machinery.
explanation: Unlike a delivery truck, factory machine depreciation is a component of product cost (Overhead).

title: Inbound Freight on Raw Materials
description: Adding the cost of shipping materials to the factory into the inventory value.
explanation: Under IAS 2, all costs to bring inventory to its present location and condition must be capitalized.

title: Material Price Variance (at Purchase)
description: Recording the difference between actual price paid and standard price allowed.
explanation: This entry highlights immediately if the purchasing department is overpaying for steel/plastic compared to the budget.

title: Material Quantity (Usage) Variance
description: Recording the excess material used in production compared to the standard requirement.
explanation: This entry highlights if the factory floor is wasting material (scrapping too much) or if the machines are inefficient.

title: Direct Labor Rate Variance
description: Recording the difference between actual wages paid and standard labor rates.
explanation: If we used cheaper, less-skilled labor than the budget planned, we record a 'Favorable' rate variance.

title: Direct Labor Efficiency Variance
description: Recording the extra hours worked compared to the standard time allowed for a job.
explanation: This is a key KPI for factory managers. It shows how much money was lost because production took longer than it should have.

title: Variable Overhead Spending Variance
description: Recording the difference between actual variable overhead costs and the amount expected for the actual hours worked.
explanation: This tells you if you paid more for electricity or lubricants per hour of machine time than you planned.

title: Variable Overhead Efficiency Variance
description: Recording the cost impact of using more or fewer machine hours than the standard allowed for production.
explanation: If your machines are slow and take longer to make a product, you use more electricity. This entry captures that inefficiency cost.

title: Fixed Overhead Budget (Spending) Variance
description: Recording the difference between actual fixed costs (like factory insurance) and the budgeted amount.
explanation: Fixed costs shouldn't change with production volume. If they do (e.g., insurance rates go up), it is captured here.

title: Fixed Overhead Volume Variance
description: Adjusting for the 'under-utilization' of the factory's capacity.
explanation: If you built a factory for 10,000 units but only made 8,000, your 'fixed cost per unit' is too high. This variance highlights that idle capacity cost.

title: Recording Normal Spoilage
description: Adding the cost of expected/unavoidable waste to the cost of the good units produced.
explanation: Normal spoilage is considered a product cost. The 'good' products absorb the cost of the 'bad' ones that are expected in a normal run.

title: Recording Abnormal Spoilage (Loss)
description: Recording a loss for unexpected waste (e.g., machine error or operator negligence).
explanation: Abnormal spoilage is NOT a product cost. It goes directly to the P&L as a separate line item so management can investigate the failure.

title: Normal Rework Costs
description: Recording the labor and material used to fix a defective unit that was caught by QC.
explanation: If rework is expected, the cost is usually charged to Overhead and spread across all products.

title: Routine Preventive Maintenance
description: Recording the cost of scheduled servicing for factory robots or assembly belts.
explanation: Routine maintenance is an indirect production cost. It is essential for keeping the factory running and is part of actual overhead.

title: Emergency Breakdown Repair
description: Recording the unplanned repair cost of a machine that halted production.
explanation: Major unplanned repairs are often expensed immediately as a period cost to highlight the financial impact of the downtime.

title: Replacement of Consumable Tooling/Molds
description: Recording the purchase of specialized molds that wear out after a set number of uses.
explanation: Molds are often 'prepaid' assets. They are expensed into Overhead based on the number of units they produce (Units of Production method).

title: Service Department Cost Allocation (Step-down)
description: Allocating the cost of the Maintenance department to the Production departments.
explanation: Maintenance doesn't 'make' anything, but its costs must be moved to the departments that actually produce units so they can be included in the product cost.

title: Inventory Write-down to Net Realizable Value (NRV)
description: Adjusting the value of finished goods when the market price falls below the production cost.
explanation: Under IAS 2, inventory must be reported at the lower of cost or NRV. If you can't sell the product for more than it cost to make, you must take the loss immediately.

title: Provision for Raw Material Obsolescence
description: Creating a reserve for materials that are expiring or no longer needed due to product design changes.
explanation: Instead of removing the material physically, we create a 'bucket' (allowance) to show that some of our stock is likely worthless.

title: Unallocated Overhead (Idle Capacity)
description: Charging fixed overhead to the P&L because production volume was significantly below normal capacity.
explanation: IAS 2 states that fixed overhead should be allocated to products based on 'normal' capacity. If the factory is half-empty, that 'wasted' rent and insurance cannot be added to the product cost; it must be expensed as a loss.

title: Capitalizing Labor for In-House Machine Build
description: Recording the cost of factory engineers building a new piece of production equipment for the company's own use.
explanation: If your own staff builds a machine, their salary for that time is not a 'period expense.' It is part of the cost of the machine and must be moved to the balance sheet.

title: Major Overhaul (Asset Life Extension)
description: Capitalizing a significant repair that increases the output or life of a machine.
explanation: Minor repairs are expensed. However, a major rebuild that makes a machine 'better than new' is capitalized and depreciated over the new remaining life.

title: Impairment of Specialized Tooling
description: Writing off the value of molds or tools because the specific product they make has been discontinued.
explanation: If a tool can only make 'Product X' and you stop selling 'Product X,' that tool is worthless regardless of its physical condition. This is a permanent loss.

title: Tolling Arrangement Revenue (Service Only)
description: Recording revenue when the factory processes raw materials owned by a customer.
explanation: In a 'Tolling' deal, the factory doesn't buy raw materials. It just sells its labor and machine time. No COGS (Inventory) is recorded, only the operating costs of the factory.

title: Purchase of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
description: Recording the purchase of safety gear (helmets, gloves, goggles) for factory workers.
explanation: Safety gear is an indirect production cost (Overhead). It is required for the factory to operate safely.

title: Product Prototype Development (Expensing Phase)
description: Recording materials and labor used to create an initial prototype before mass production.
explanation: Unless specific capitalization criteria are met, the cost of 'testing and trying' new designs is expensed immediately as R&D.

title: Recognizing Byproduct Inventory at Harvest
description: Recording the value of a byproduct when it is produced, reducing the cost of the main product.
explanation: Instead of waiting to sell the byproduct, some factories recognize its value immediately to lower the 'net cost' of the main items they are making.

title: Outsourced Manufacturing (Contract Manufacturer)
description: Recording the cost when an external factory (CMO) produces the product for you.
explanation: Even if you don't make it, you own the materials. When the external factory finishes, you convert your raw materials plus their service fee into Finished Goods.

title: Year-End Variance Proration
description: Allocating variances across WIP, Finished Goods, and COGS rather than just dumping them in COGS.
explanation: If variances are 'material,' auditors require you to spread the extra cost across all inventory that was touched by those costs, not just the items already sold.

title: Inter-Plant WIP Transfer
description: Moving semi-finished goods from one company factory to another for final processing.
explanation: This tracks the physical location of value within the company's internal supply chain.

title: Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO) Accrual
description: Recording the future cost of safely dismantling heavy machinery at the end of its life.
explanation: If a specialized machine requires a $2,000 clean-up cost in 10 years, you must capitalize that cost today and depreciate it over the machine's life.

title: Vendor Managed Inventory (Consignment Buy)
description: Recording the purchase of raw materials that were already sitting in your warehouse but owned by the vendor.
explanation: In VMI, you only 'buy' the material and record the liability the moment you pull the item from the shelf to use it in production.

title: Environmental Penalty (Non-Deductible)
description: Recording a government fine for a factory emission violation.
explanation: While this is a factory expense, it is usually disallowed for tax purposes. It is a 'Period Cost' and never part of the product cost.

title: Provision for Product Recall
description: Estimating and recording the cost of a future product recall based on a known defect.
explanation: Following the Prudence principle, if you know a batch is bad, you must record the estimated cost of retrieving and fixing it immediately.

title: Government Energy Efficiency Grant
description: Recording a grant received for installing solar panels or high-efficiency motors in the factory.
explanation: Grants for assets are recognized as revenue over the same period the asset is depreciated, to match the benefit to the cost.

title: Unrealized Intercompany Profit Elimination
description: Eliminating the 'markup' added when the manufacturing branch sells inventory to the retail branch of the same company.
explanation: If the factory sells a unit to its own store for $10 profit, that profit isn't 'real' until the store sells it to a real customer. This entry 'cleans' the inventory value for consolidation.

title: Royalty for Patented Production Process
description: Recording a fee paid to a 3rd party for every unit produced using their technology.
explanation: Licensing fees for a production method are part of the 'Indirect Product Cost' and flow through overhead into the unit cost.

title: Physical Count Adjustment (Shortage)
description: Adjusting the digital system to match the physical count in the warehouse.
explanation: The 'Moment of Truth' where errors, theft, or evaporation are recorded as a loss to ensure the Balance Sheet is accurate.

title: Machine Installation & Testing Costs
description: Capitalizing the technical labor and materials used to set up a new production line.
explanation: Under IAS 16, all costs to bring an asset to 'working condition for its intended use' must be added to the asset value.

title: New Machine Training (Expensing)
description: Recording the cost of training staff to use a new robotic arm.
explanation: Crucial distinction: You capitalize the machine setup, but you generally MUST expense the staff training. Knowledge cannot be 'owned' as a tangible asset.

title: Excise Duty on Production
description: Recording the tax owed to the government for producing specific goods (e.g., tobacco, fuel, luxury items).
explanation: In many countries, tax is triggered at the 'Moment of Manufacture,' not the moment of sale. This tax becomes a part of the product's cost.

title: Sale of Excess Carbon Credits
description: Recording revenue when a clean factory sells its unused pollution permits.
explanation: If a factory operates below its emission limit, it can monetize its efficiency. This is a non-core revenue stream.

title: Capitalizing Development Costs (Success)
description: Moving costs from the P&L to the Balance Sheet once a new product becomes commercially viable.
explanation: Under IFRS (IAS 38), once a product reaches 'Technical Feasibility,' you stop expensing and start capitalizing.

title: Night Shift Premium (Labor Cost)
description: Recording the extra wages paid to workers on the 2nd or 3rd shift.
explanation: Overtime and shift premiums paid to direct laborers are part of the 'Direct Labor' component of product cost.

title: Closing Over-applied Overhead
description: Adjusting COGS when the factory was more efficient than the standard estimate.
explanation: The final closing entry to zero out the temporary overhead accounts and reduce COGS for the 'savings' achieved.

title: Dine-In Cash Sale
description: Recording a standard table sale paid in cash at the end of the meal.
explanation: The full amount received includes 16% VAT (Jordan). Revenue is only the pre-tax amount. The tax collected belongs to the ISTD and is a liability until remitted.

title: Dine-In Credit/Debit Card Sale
description: Recording a table sale paid by card, with a POS processing fee deducted by the bank.
explanation: Always record the full sale price as revenue. The bank processing fee (typically 2.5%) is a separate selling expense, not a reduction of revenue.

title: Takeaway / To-Go Order (Cash)
description: Recording a customer who orders and pays at the counter for food to take away.
explanation: Takeaway revenue is recorded at the point of handover to the customer. VAT applies the same way as dine-in sales.

title: Split Bill (Part Cash, Part Card)
description: Recording a table where two guests split the bill — one pays cash, the other pays by card.
explanation: Split payments require you to record each payment method separately while keeping the total revenue and VAT intact as one transaction.

title: POS Void / Cancelled Order
description: Reversing a sale that was incorrectly entered into the POS system before payment was collected.
explanation: A void cancels a transaction as if it never happened. It is a direct reversal of the original entry. This is different from a refund, which happens after payment has been collected.

title: Service Charge Collected from Customer
description: Recording the 10% service charge added to the bill that belongs to the staff.
explanation: Service charges are NOT restaurant revenue. They are collected on behalf of the staff and held as a liability until distributed via payroll.

title: Service Charge Distribution to Staff
description: Paying out the accumulated monthly service charge pool to waitstaff and kitchen team.
explanation: This clears the liability created every time a service charge was collected. The distribution can be cash or added to the payroll run.

title: Cash Tips Left on Table
description: Recording cash tips left by customers directly on the table for the waiting staff.
explanation: Tips belong to the staff, not the restaurant. The cash is held briefly as a liability until handed to the team at the end of the shift.

title: Card Tips Added to POS Bill
description: Recording a tip the customer adds to their card payment at the end of the meal.
explanation: Card tips come in net of the bank's processing fee. The restaurant absorbs the fee but must still pass the full $10 tip to the staff.

title: Customer Refund (Cash)
description: Issuing a cash refund to a dissatisfied customer for a meal that was returned.
explanation: Using Sales Returns (not a direct debit to Revenue) lets you track the volume of refunds as a KPI. VAT must also be reversed since no taxable supply ultimately occurred.

title: Manager Comp / Complimentary Meal
description: A manager approves a free meal for a VIP guest or to resolve a complaint.
explanation: You do NOT record the menu price as a sale. You move the cost of the raw ingredients out of inventory and into a marketing/guest relations expense account.

title: Customer Loyalty Points Earned (Revenue Deferral)
description: Deferring a portion of a sale's value for loyalty points earned by the customer at the time of purchase.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, if points have a standalone value, you cannot recognize the full revenue at time of sale. The points portion is deferred as a liability until redeemed or expired.

title: Customer Loyalty Points Redeemed
description: A customer uses accumulated points to pay for part of their meal.
explanation: When points are redeemed, the deferred liability is converted to earned revenue. The remaining balance is collected in cash.

title: Coupon / Voucher Redemption
description: A customer presents a printed or digital coupon for a discount on their meal.
explanation: Recording the full sale and a separate discount lets you analyze exactly how much revenue was given away in promotions without distorting your gross revenue figure.

title: Staff Meal (50% Discount Policy)
description: An employee eats at the restaurant and pays half price per the staff discount policy.
explanation: The cash portion is standard revenue. The discounted 50% is an employee benefit expense — the restaurant absorbs this cost. No VAT is charged on staff meals in most Jordan interpretations.

title: Catering Order Advance Deposit
description: Receiving a deposit from a corporate client for a large catering event next month.
explanation: The food has not been prepared yet, so this is a liability. Revenue is only recognized when the catering event is completed and the food is delivered.

title: Catering Event Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue when the catering order is delivered and the event takes place.
explanation: The deposit is cleared, the remaining balance is billed as a receivable, and the full revenue (net of VAT) is recognized on the event date.

title: Sale of Restaurant Gift Card
description: A customer purchases a prepaid gift card to give as a present.
explanation: A gift card is not a sale. It is a liability because the restaurant owes the holder food worth JOD 50. Revenue is only recognized when the card is spent at the counter.

title: Gift Card Redemption
description: A customer uses a gift card to pay for their meal.
explanation: When the card is used, the liability converts to earned revenue. VAT is recognized at the point of use, not at the point of sale of the card.

title: Gift Card Breakage (Unredeemed Balance)
description: Recognizing revenue from gift card balances that are statistically unlikely to ever be redeemed.
explanation: Using historical data, if 5% of gift cards are never redeemed, you can recognize that portion as Other Income over time. This is called 'breakage' under IFRS 15.

title: Purchase of Raw Ingredients (Cash)
description: Buying fresh vegetables, meat, and dairy from a market vendor for cash.
explanation: Raw ingredients are assets until used in cooking. They only become an expense (Cost of Sales) when they are consumed to produce a meal that is sold.

title: Purchase of Raw Ingredients (On Credit)
description: Receiving a weekly delivery of ingredients from a supplier with a 30-day payment term.
explanation: Purchasing on credit creates a liability. The inventory is an asset the moment it arrives at the restaurant, regardless of when you pay for it.

title: Purchase of Beverages / Alcohol
description: Buying soft drinks, juices, and beverages from a distributor.
explanation: Beverage inventory is tracked separately from food to help calculate the Beverage Cost % (a key F&B KPI). VAT on the purchase is a recoverable input tax.

title: Inbound Freight on Ingredient Delivery
description: Paying a delivery fee for ingredients to be brought from a wholesaler to the restaurant.
explanation: Under IAS 2, all costs required to bring inventory to its present location must be capitalized into the inventory value — including delivery fees.

title: Return of Spoiled Goods to Supplier
description: Sending back a delivery of tomatoes that arrived damaged, and receiving a credit note.
explanation: The credit note reduces what you owe the supplier and removes the damaged goods from your inventory balance. No expense is recorded.

title: Early Payment Discount from Supplier
description: Paying a supplier invoice early and receiving a 2% cash discount.
explanation: The discount reduces the effective cost of the inventory. In a perpetual system, the saving is credited back to inventory rather than being recorded as income.

title: Daily Food Cost (Inventory to COGS)
